description: Phrases for socializing, making friends, and everyday interactions

items.english: Would you like to go for coffee?
items.notes: 'Haluaisitko' is the conditional of 'haluta' with second person '-t' and question suffix '-ko'. 'Kahville' is the allative of 'kahvi' (coffee). Coffee culture is huge in Finland — Finns are among the world's top coffee drinkers.

'Mitä kuuluu' literally means 'what is heard/what belongs'. This is the standard Finnish greeting equivalent to 'how are you?'. Common replies: 'Hyvää, kiitos!' (Good, thanks!) or 'Ihan hyvää' (Pretty good).
'Mukava' means 'nice/pleasant'. 'Tavata' means 'to meet'. In Finland, people typically shake hands when meeting for the first time. Finns tend to introduce themselves with just their first name.

'Mistä' is the elative of 'mikä' (from where). 'Kotoisin' means 'originally from' (derived from 'koti', home). This is a common conversation starter. Answer: 'Olen kotoisin...' + country/city in the elative case.
Follow with your country or city in the elative case (-sta/-stä): 'Englannista' (from England), 'Amerikasta' (from America), 'Australiasta' (from Australia). Cities: 'Lontoosta' (from London).
'Puhutko' is from 'puhua' (to speak) with second person '-t' and question suffix '-ko'. 'Englantia' is the partitive of 'englanti'. Most Finns speak excellent English, especially younger generations.
'Vähän' means 'a little'. 'Suomea' is the partitive of 'suomi' (Finnish). Finns are typically very appreciative when foreigners try to speak Finnish, even just a little.

'Anteeksi' means 'sorry/excuse me'. 'En ymmärrä' is the negative of 'ymmärtää' (to understand). 'En' is the first person negative verb. This is essential when you need someone to repeat or switch to English.

'Voitko' is from 'voida' (can) with second person '-t' and question suffix '-ko'. 'Hitaammin' is the comparative adverb of 'hitaasti' (slowly). Very useful when learning Finnish, as spoken Finnish can be quite fast.
'Tarkoittaa' means 'to mean'. Useful when encountering unfamiliar Finnish words or signs. You can also point at text and ask this question.

'Pidetään' is the passive/first person plural imperative of 'pitää' (to keep). 'Yhteyttä' is the partitive of 'yhteys' (connection/contact). A warm way to end a conversation with someone you'd like to stay connected with.

'Hauskaa' is the partitive of 'hauska' (fun/nice). 'Viikonloppua' is the partitive of 'viikonloppu' (weekend), a compound from 'viikko' (week) + 'loppu' (end). Common Friday farewell among colleagues and friends.

'Onneksi' is the translative of 'onni' (happiness/luck). 'Olkoon' is the imperative of 'olla' (to be). Used for graduations, weddings, promotions, and other achievements. Can be shortened to 'Onnittelut!' (Congratulations!).

'Syntymäpäivää' is the partitive of 'syntymäpäivä' (birthday), a compound from 'syntymä' (birth) + 'päivä' (day). Finnish birthday traditions include the birthday person often providing cake ('kakku') at work or school.

'Joulua' is the partitive of 'joulu' (Christmas). Christmas is the most important holiday in Finland. 'Joulupukki' (Santa Claus) is said to live in Korvatunturi, Finnish Lapland. Christmas Eve ('jouluaatto') is the main celebration day.

'Uutta' is the partitive of 'uusi' (new). 'Vuotta' is the partitive of 'vuosi' (year). New Year's Eve ('uudenvuodenaatto') is celebrated with fireworks throughout Finland. Tin casting ('tinannvalanta') is a traditional activity.

'Tervetuloa' is a compound from 'terve' (healthy/well) + 'tuloa' (coming, partitive of 'tulo'). Used to welcome guests to your home, workplace, or Finland. You'll see this on doormats and welcome signs.
'Voitko' is the informal 'can you' from 'voida'. 'Auttaa' means 'to help'. 'Minua' is the partitive of 'minä'. This is the informal version; use 'Voitteko' for formal/polite situations.

'Avusta' is the elative case of 'apu' (help). 'Kiitos' + elative is the standard Finnish construction for 'thank you for...'. Always good to express gratitude, even in the famously reserved Finnish culture.

'Nähdään' is the passive form of 'nähdä' (to see), used as 'we'll see each other'. 'Pian' means 'soon'. This is a warm, casual farewell. Other variations: 'Nähdään huomenna!' (See you tomorrow!), 'Nähdään ensi viikolla!' (See you next week!).
